items.q: What does a 'large scale' map mean?
items.correct: A large-scale map covers a small area in high detail (e.g. 1:25,000 OS map of one town); a small-scale map covers a large area in less detail (e.g. 1:1,000,000 world atlas).
items.misconception: A large-scale map shows a large area.
items.random_wrong: Large scale means the map is physically big.
items.why: This is counter-intuitive but mathematically logical: 1/25,000 is a larger fraction than 1/1,000,000. Students reverse the terminology and consistently mark themselves down on map skill questions.

items.q: What is the difference between a choropleth and an isoline map?
items.correct: Choropleths use coloured/shaded areas to show data values per region (e.g. population density by county); isoline maps use lines connecting points of equal value (e.g. contours, isobars).
items.misconception: Choropleth and isoline maps are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Choropleth maps are for showing churches and isoline maps are for showing roads.
items.why: They suit different data types: choropleth for area-based discrete data; isoline for continuous variables. Choosing the wrong one misrepresents the data — a common A-level skills question.

items.q: Why is the urban heat island effect strongest at night?
items.correct: Concrete and brick released their stored daytime heat slowly into the night air, while rural areas cool rapidly through radiative loss — widening the urban-rural temperature gap after sunset.
items.misconception: Urban heat islands are strongest at midday because cities are sunniest then.
items.random_wrong: Urban heat islands only happen in summer.
items.why: Thermal mass of urban materials is the key — they buffer city temperature. At night, this buffering creates the largest urban-rural difference, sometimes 5-10 degrees C in major cities.

items.q: How many countries are in Africa?
items.why: It's 54. “100” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is the largest hot desert in the world?
items.why: Correct answer: Sahara. “Gobi” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Africa's population is roughly how many billion?
items.why: Exact answer: 1. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
